sections.title: 🧒 The Story
sections.rawMarkdown:
```
Without CI/CD:
→ developer merges code
→ someone builds manually on laptop
→ someone SSHs into server
→ restarts app and hopes it works

Problems:
→ no repeatability
→ no audit trail
→ deploys depend on one hero engineer
→ rollback is stressful and slow

CI/CD turns releases into a defined system:
commit → build → test → deploy → verify → rollback if needed
```

---

blocks.content:
Without CI/CD:
→ developer merges code
→ someone builds manually on laptop
→ someone SSHs into server
→ restarts app and hopes it works

Problems:
→ no repeatability
→ no audit trail
→ deploys depend on one hero engineer
→ rollback is stressful and slow

CI/CD turns releases into a defined system:
commit → build → test → deploy → verify → rollback if needed

revisionPoints: → restarts app and hopes it works
→ deploys depend on one hero engineer

sections.title: 🔁 Core Services
sections.rawMarkdown:
### CodePipeline
```
CodePipeline = orchestration layer
→ defines stages: Source → Build → Test → Deploy
→ integrates with GitHub, CodeBuild, CodeDeploy, CloudFormation
→ visual pipeline state and history
```

### CodeBuild
```
CodeBuild = managed build runner
→ compiles code, runs tests, builds Docker images
→ ephemeral environment per build
→ buildspec.yml defines commands

Example buildspec phases:
install  → dependencies
pre_build → auth/login/setup
build    → tests + package/build
post_build → push image / create artifacts
```

### CodeDeploy
```
CodeDeploy = deployment engine
→ deploy to EC2, ECS, Lambda
→ rolling, blue/green, canary styles depending on target
→ lifecycle hooks for validation before/after traffic shift
```

---

blocks.content:
CodePipeline = orchestration layer
→ defines stages: Source → Build → Test → Deploy
→ integrates with GitHub, CodeBuild, CodeDeploy, CloudFormation
→ visual pipeline state and history

CodeBuild = managed build runner

→ buildspec.yml defines commands

Example buildspec phases:

pre_build → auth/login/setup
build    → tests + package/build
post_build → push image / create artifacts

CodeDeploy = deployment engine
→ deploy to EC2, ECS, Lambda
→ rolling, blue/green, canary styles depending on target
→ lifecycle hooks for validation before/after traffic shift

sections.title: 🚦 Deployment Strategies
sections.rawMarkdown:
```
ROLLING:
→ replace instances/tasks gradually
→ lower extra cost
→ slower rollback than true blue/green

BLUE/GREEN:
→ old version and new version run side by side
→ shift traffic when green proves healthy
→ fastest rollback: move traffic back to blue

CANARY:
→ small percentage first
→ good for catching production-only issues early
```

---

blocks.content:
ROLLING:
→ replace instances/tasks gradually
→ lower extra cost
→ slower rollback than true blue/green

BLUE/GREEN:

→ fastest rollback: move traffic back to blue

CANARY:
→ small percentage first
→ good for catching production-only issues early

sections.title: 💡 CI/CD Best Practices
sections.rawMarkdown:
```
✅ small frequent releases
✅ automated tests before deploy
✅ immutable artifacts (same artifact moves across environments)
✅ separate build from deploy
✅ health checks and smoke tests after deploy
✅ automated rollback criteria on alarms
✅ infrastructure changes go through pipeline too, not only app code
```

---

blocks.content:
✅ small frequent releases
✅ automated tests before deploy
✅ immutable artifacts (same artifact moves across environments)
✅ separate build from deploy
✅ health checks and smoke tests after deploy
✅ automated rollback criteria on alarms
✅ infrastructure changes go through pipeline too, not only app code

revisionPoints: ✅ immutable artifacts (same artifact moves across environments)
✅ health checks and smoke tests after deploy
✅ infrastructure changes go through pipeline too, not only app code

sections.title: 🎯 Interview Q&A — CI/CD
sections.rawMarkdown:
**Q: What makes a deployment pipeline production-grade?**
```
"Not just automation. A production-grade pipeline has safety.

I look for:
→ immutable artifacts
→ automated tests
→ environment promotion path
→ approvals where needed
→ health verification after deployment
→ rollback mechanism tied to alarms

A script that copies files to prod is automation.
A pipeline that reduces change risk is CI/CD."
```

**Q: How do you reduce deployment risk for critical services?**
```
"Use progressive delivery.

My preferred sequence:
→ deploy to staging with realistic tests
→ blue/green or canary in production
→ watch latency, errors, saturation, business KPIs
→ auto-rollback if thresholds breach

Senior delivery is about limiting blast radius, not just making deployment faster."
```

**Q: Why separate CodeBuild from CodeDeploy instead of doing everything in one script?**
```
"Because build and deploy solve different problems.

Build should create a tested immutable artifact.
Deploy should promote that exact artifact into environments.

If you rebuild per environment, you lose traceability and increase drift.
Separation gives you reproducibility, auditing, and safer rollback."
```

---

sections.title: 🔗 Connects To
sections.rawMarkdown:
```
CodePipeline ← GitHub/CodeCommit: source changes
CodePipeline → CodeBuild: build and test
CodePipeline → CloudFormation/CodeDeploy/ECS/Lambda: deploy targets
CodeBuild → ECR/S3: store built artifacts and images
CodeDeploy → ALB/Lambda/ECS: traffic shifting and rollout control
CI/CD → CloudWatch alarms: deployment verification and rollback triggers

NEXT: Well-Architected + Architecture Patterns
How to judge whether the whole system is actually well designed.
```

---

blocks.content:
CodePipeline ← GitHub/CodeCommit: source changes
CodePipeline → CodeBuild: build and test
CodePipeline → CloudFormation/CodeDeploy/ECS/Lambda: deploy targets
CodeBuild → ECR/S3: store built artifacts and images
CodeDeploy → ALB/Lambda/ECS: traffic shifting and rollout control
CI/CD → CloudWatch alarms: deployment verification and rollback triggers

NEXT: Well-Architected + Architecture Patterns
How to judge whether the whole system is actually well designed.
